speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four, interview with Nechama Shneorson, November 16th, 1995. Charlotte Rettinger interviewer, Flushing, New York. So you were hit on the head, and when you became conscious again--

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I didn't find any more my mother and sister standing there or anybody any more. They were taken away from us, which took me probably over a year and a half later to find out what really happened to them. They were taken to Auschwitz, to the crematoriums. And this was-- that was the end of my family starting to fall apart. At this point, I was left only with my sister in Stutthof, not even being together with her closely. I knew she was there because she was in different wagons that-- when we were brought into Stutthof. We lost each other's places and we lost each other. We didn't know who-- where she was. She didn't know where I was-- different barracks, different places-- and we lost each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: So actually, I was left alone. And around me, four or five other girlfriends that were left without mothers. So we tried to be together as much as we could and help each other whatever we could.

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple of days later, we were taken out of Stutthof. They called it Arbeitslager, which it means to a working camp. And all I had on me was-- since they took us out from the showers first when we came there-- I had a silk blouse, a skirt, no socks, no stockings, made out of wood a pair of sandals, and a very light coat. This clothing and this place where we are-- where we were is just good enough for summer. And we are talking now about getting closer to winter, and it started to be very, very cold. I mean, cold is not a word. Any of this kind of clothes was not fitted for this kind of weather. We were taken to a place which is called Torun. We were given--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me, what's that name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Torun. That's a Polish little city, nearby a city-- nearby the city by name Torun.

speaker: interviewer
text: T-o-r-u-n?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were given-- I have a problem to remember that name for it-- where like soldiers live in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a barrack, in a-- oh, God, I'm mixing the name. Made of fabric, a little--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, a tent.

speaker: interviewee
text: A tent. Wow. A tent, that's where we were living in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you are in the Arbeitslager?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were living in tents. We were given-- in the morning, we were given they just-- they said-- they called it coffee and one slice of bread. It was not coffee. It was just dirty water, and that slice of bread you swallowed in a half a second, and then of course you had to wait a full day to get your evening's soup what they called, which was contained of water, one potato maybe if you find it, if you could find it, or another kind of vegetable. And this was our daily rations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Our work was to dig ditches-- ditches? For the army. Ditches? Ditches. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ditches. Ditches. One good thing worked out of that. My arms were very strong and my hands became very large. 12, 13-year-old girl with a big shovel to shovel out a meter and 80, the deep-- as deep as that, to bring out all the sand and, and stones out of there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was very sad going into that tent in the evening, talking to your friends the same age, crying where our mothers and fathers and sisters and brothers are. My only prayer used to be, wherever you are, you should not suffer and struggle as much as I am because I'll take everything on me so you shouldn't be punished as much. And hopefully I'll see you again at home. That was my prayer in the morning and a prayer night, and the girls together with me used to pray the same thing. Whatever has to happen to us, to all of us, let it be to me, but you shouldn't suffer. It started to get so cold that mornings we used to get up, we couldn't move away from our tent because our hair used to get frozen to the tent. Snow started to fall, and the, the cold was impossible to take. If you didn't move, many people fell actually like a piece of ice and they died, frozen, while they were standing. You could see a body standing there and in a second it was laying on the floor like a piece of ice that fell, frozen to death. Near me used to be a woman that used to say, don't stand for a second. Keep moving, walk do something, because you couldn't even take apart your hands to take the shovel. It was free-- this is how bad it was. Some kids used to escape from there if the German didn't see you and run into a house and ask for a piece of bread or soup or anything they had to warm up for a while. And some of them got it, and some of them used to be thrown out of there. They were afraid to keep you there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I noticed that a couple of times that somebody's doing it, but I never had the nerve to do it. By that time, my wooden sandals fell apart completely, and I was actually walking barefoot in the snow. And it's hard to describe the pain walking around that way.

speaker: interviewee
text: The worse part-- worse than that was yet that we didn't have water to wash, and we didn't wash for weeks and months by now. And-- I'm going to say something terrible-- we were all covered with lice, and the pain of that was probably worse than the cold, what lice can do to you. We tried to wash our heads to get rid of the lice. Believe it or not, in-- with the hot water that we had wasn't even so hot, so the five girls that we've been together, we shared each-- with each other the water one day, and one used to wash her hair out. The second day, I used to give mine to the other girl. And so during the week, each one of us had a chance to wash once at least a week our hair. But then of course it didn't help because we didn't have soap. It was just no, no help to us at all. When it got very, very bitter cold, they finally took us out from those tents, and they brought us into a place what used to be a mill-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] flour mill-- and they made those wooden places that we could sleep together, just some plain wood. There was nothing else, not a blanket or, God forbid, anything, not at all, just lay there on the wood. But at least we were warm, close, our bodies next to each other, and we were not as freezing as in those tents. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How long can one live on one slice of bread and a little bit of water? People started to die of starvation. People started to get very sick, and there was no help, medical help, so they just shoot you when they notice that the person cannot work anymore. And the camp started to get slower-- smaller and smaller.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, when I felt I am at the end of my strength, hardly walking out from the camp going to work, I decided I'm going to do just like somebody else did, is try to escape, and run into a house, maybe beg for something. And when I notice that a German wasn't watching me I did. And I came into a house and I asked them just leave me-- I'm not asking for anything. Just let me sit here and warm. I'm not-- don't give me anything. I don't want anything. Just let me sit here, and they did, and they really didn't give me nothing, not a piece of bread or a bite of bread. Just let me sit there. And in the warm, I felt like I'm back to the world that I haven't seen for years now. Going back-- it was time to go back because I had to go back to camp-- I did not watch so carefully, and the German noticed me. So he ran over, me-- seeing me coming from the outside, and he beat me up on my back. I got 25 bad lashes all over my back. He beat me so badly that he broke my cup. It wasn't a cup. It was made out of tin, tin, where I used to get my soup or my water in it. He broke that on my back because it was hanging right in, in-- on a-- on a string. it was hanging like that on a-- he broke that. Of course, they grabbed me. My friends grabbed me and hardly, hardly walking on my own feet, we came back to camp. And during the night I picked myself up a little bit and I said, if this was going to happen to me, I'm going to do that tomorrow again, but he's not going to catch me again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure enough, on the second morning, we woke up. We walked out to the place. I escaped again, and I came into a house, and I said to them, I'm hungry and I'm barefoot. And I cannot continue working to make the Germans happy. Can you just help me with some food? And I'll do everything you want me into this house. Just let me stay with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll feed your cows and I'll feed your pigs and I'll wash your floors. I'll sew it for you. I'll do anything. Just let me stay with you. And they did, and I was working there a full day and every potato I threw into the cows or to the pigs, the second potato was in my mouth. I filled myself up with so much of it, I cannot tell you how many, and packed my pockets full of them. By the time I had the evening, I had to go back, made sure, first of all, that the German will not see me, but I had even in my pockets some potatoes I could give my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I did it every day for two weeks, and I build myself up. I became the, the maid of this house. Just imagine me, 13 years old, skinny, hardly even to know what I look like, but I fed everybody. I washed. I scrubbed the floors. I washed their laundry. I even fixed their-- my mother was a very good teacher-- their socks. You know those days they used to fix their socks? Darn, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did that. And the only one thing she was begging me, the, the hostess, that I shouldn't take anything with me, because if they will find out where I'm bringing the food, they're going to be killed. Of course, I never listened to her, and always-- my pockets were always with bread and potatoes, and I brought them back to my friends. If I wouldn't have done that, I wouldn't be sitting here and talking to you today. That picked me up to have the death road that we had to go right after that. Because one morning we woke up and they didn't take us to work, but we had to escape from the Russians. They were starting to come closer to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was already in the early days of January 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Still bitter winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bad winter. I was walking at this point with two left man's shoes. That's the only one thing the woman could give me, but I wasn't barefoot. And from half of my coat I sewed myself-- I made myself gloves, and some pieces to put on on my legs. So until today I have on my knee a sign where I had a frost bite. So I on my knees have those frostbites left, a sign. But otherwise-- when they took us on that march and the Russians started to bomb the city where we were, we had to cross a bridge to go. And the Russians were coming over there with airplanes and bombing the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were standing on the bridge and begging, why don't you just throw one bomb on us to finish us in one second instead of continue struggling with our lives like that? But it's funny so that they did not. The Germans ran away from us, and we were just standing there in the middle of the bridge and waiting if bombs should hit us, but not one did, and we had to continue walking.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was probably the luckiest of everybody because at this point those two weeks that I ate well and I was in a warm house, I really didn't feel the pain as bad. But to watch everybody what was going on around me was a disaster. I wanted to die with them together. I was ashamed that my stomach was full because they died on the roads star-- from starvation, from cold. And if they fell fainting so the Germans killed them right there and then. They didn't have-- they wouldn't let us carry them. So we left bodies on the road, tens and tens like that, hundreds. We were on the road for four days. One evening they took us into, to the woods, the Germans, and they said, listen, your time is just come. We leave you here. We cannot stay with you anymore. We leave you here, and you do whatever you want to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: What could we have done? Nobody had the strength to do, and nobody knew where we are. So we stayed there and early in the morning the Russians came there. The first Russian that I saw on a beautiful horse, and if I tell you of the couple of hundred people that were left, everybody falling on the floor kissing the horse's legs and the Russian, and screaming out of hysterics, thanking them for coming for our liberation. That's a picture that I will never in my life get out of my mind, never. I turn around and I said to myself, so what good is it? Where's my mother? Where's my father? Where are my sisters? So what am I here now, left alive? I don't even know if my family is alive. So who wants to live? I really had no need for it. Life was not important to me at this point any more.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did what everybody else did. They told us where to go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They brought us into a place, a city, which was a beautiful, beautiful city. It was called one of the most gorgeous cities in Poland, Ciechocinek. They called it one of the most prominent [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] places where people, very rich people, used to come for vacations, beautiful hotels. And they said they're going to feed us there, which they did. They fed us. We could wash up a little bit in those hotels. We stayed there for a week, picked ourselves up a little bit, cleaned our lice out of our bodies. And then came in a report that we have to go with the Russians to do some work for them. We-- they were collecting all these livestock from the fields. They were running away from the farms because of the bombing. We have to put them together. We have to bring them together in a certain place, which was in the farms, and they took them and moved them into Russia. So we were stationed in a farm. I was there together with my-- some of my friends, elderly people, whatever was left, and some of my friends were with me. And we worked for the Russians six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Feeding the cows-- when it was? That was-- we were liberated January 23rd, 1945. We're six months from there on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Till June. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So during that period of the six months, we did everything we could to help the Russians, to bring the cows and horses together in the farms.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still around that area-- I can't remember the names of these-- of the little farms there. But the Russian army was around us. They were very helpful. They didn't touch us. We were kids. We told them the stories of our-- from what, what happened to us. They were very moved by all this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you spoke Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that-- all of you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. And, and by the one-- about six months later-- we actually moved from one place to another. We did not stay in one place. We finished in one place that kind of job. They took us-- they told us to go to another farm where, again, more cows were supposed to be put together and brought together. The cows, that they couldn't walk. We had to take care of them in the farm itself. And one day came in the manager said that we are done with our job, and some of those cows that cannot be taken have to be taken to slaughter, so we should take them to a certain place. They're going to be slaughtered there, and we will be able to go back home. They knew that where we coming from, and they are very happy to announce to us that we finally can go home, which goes-- which is in Lithuania. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One of my girlfriends and myself-- at this point we learned how to ride horses, because you have to know how to ride a horse on a farm-- and we were moving those cows towards the place where they told us to go, which was another little city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we met on the road a woman, and she asked us, are you Jewish? Yes. I live-- I'm also on a farm like that, also deliberated by the Russians, and there's a man that came in and he came all the way from Germany. He's looking for his family. And he is-- he's looking for them, a wi-- his wife and his daughter. And he's-- where is he? So she said, he's there. Do you want to go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The two of us ran to see him, and don't you believe this was my girlfriend's father, which was my sister's father-in-law. Now he came from Dachau when he was deliberated by the Germ-- by the American army. And he immediately started to find out, looking for where women and children were left and where they were. And from place to place he was told where to go to keep looking for them. With 300 kilometers walking and looking and searching, and he found us there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- this is-- this is-- nobody did anything like it. And when the two of us fell on him, and it was just unbelievable, an unbelievable, unbelievable feeling. And I'm crying and screaming, how wonderful to see him. And I don't see my father near him, and this was the first time that the, the-- it occurred to me if he-- my father is not near him. That means my father is not alive because my father would have done the same thing looking for us. That was the-- a terrible thought for me, the realization actually that my father is not alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I did ask him, so where is my father? He says, well, you should be very happy to know that he wasn't killed. He actually died. I said, how did he die? From starvation? Yes. I said, I wish he would have been killed instead of living all the way till to starve to death. I know what that looks to be starved to death. I've seen them around me all the time. It's terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: Being as it may, he said to us, listen kids, you cannot stay here with the Russians. They want you to take you back to Russia. You're not going back to Russia. Let's go, pick up mother and we, we are going to escape the Russians, because we are not going to go back to Russia. This is the difference between a mature man thinking what it is all about in the world already happening and children like us that we were looking forward to go back to Lithuania, to Kovno. Maybe we'll find family there-- childish fantasy. The Russians were very moved that we found each other when we came back to our place, to our [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] building there. And mother was there, my girlfriend's mother. To see that was unbelievable. And the second day he said to us, not say a word because they're not going to let you do it. We'll just tell them that we're going to look for more family. At the end when we'll find them, we will come back to Lithuania. And that's what we did. And we got the first train we could. Instead of going towards Lithuania, we went actually further in to going west. And we wind up to be in Lodz, which is one of the largest cities in Poland, and we found really a lot of crowds already, people that were deliberated. And they started to try to look for each other's families. There was a lot of committees of people that they try to send letters out, who and who is where, and you just put your name on so we could find each other. My girlfriend-- my sister's father-in-law left us there and then he said, I'm going to look for my sister, because at this point when we got there, we found that my sister is alive too. That she was also taken by the Russians also to work in a camp-- in another farm. And he went from place to place and he found her, and he brought her there. He brought her to us. That was the first time I met-- I saw her again after this whole ordeal with the concentration camps. We found each other. She was without hair. She had typhoid and to see each other all over again was probably the most beautiful moment of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sister is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the oldest of my sisters because the middle one was burned in the ghetto, and the little one was taken away with my mother. So we-- the two of us understood that this is it. There's no-- nobody else left in my family. So she was-- she was married already, and I didn't know where her husband is yet. We found out that he is-- he escaped the ghetto and he was alive in Kovno after hiding in the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll continue on the next tape.
